topic: work introduction — residential projects feeling empty
facts_disclosed: Works on residential architecture projects at her firm
Lives in Santiago — apartment in Barrio Yungay near her projects

turns.content: I have been staring at floor plans all afternoon and I think my eyes have forgotten how to see anything beautiful. can you help me think through something about my work, I need a different perspective
Of course, I'm happy to help. What's going on with the floor plans -- what kind of project are you working on?

residential. it is always residential these days. my firm has been doing high-end houses mostly, clients in Vitacura and Lo Barnechea who want clean lines and imported marble and everything looking like it belongs in a magazine. and I do it, I do it well, but my soul is somewhere else entirely, you know

That sounds like a real tension between what pays the bills and what actually drives you creatively. When you say your soul is somewhere else -- where is it? What kind of work would you rather be doing?

restoration. heritage buildings, the ones that carry memory in their walls. there is a Neruda line — 'I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees' — and that is what I want to do with old buildings. not demolish, not renovate into something unrecognizable, but bring them back to what they were meant to be. let them bloom again

That's a beautiful way to frame it. Heritage restoration is a very different discipline from residential design, though. Are you an architect by training? And is there a specific type of heritage building that pulls at you?

yes I am an architect, and the buildings that pull at me are the ones with stories layered into them. I live in Barrio Yungay in Santiago, and every morning when I walk to get coffee I pass facades from the 1800s that are crumbling. literally crumbling. and meanwhile I am spending my days designing kitchen islands for people who will use them twice a year for dinner parties. there is something obscene about it

Barrio Yungay has incredible architecture -- a lot of it neoclassical and Art Nouveau if I'm remembering right. So you're living surrounded by exactly the kind of buildings you want to work on, but your professional work takes you in the opposite direction. Have you explored whether there's a way to transition, even partially? Some architects split their practice between commercial work and passion projects.

that is the dream, to split the practice. the residential work is the load-bearing wall — take it away and the whole financial structure collapses. but I need at least a window, a small opening toward the work that matters. I have been thinking about approaching the municipality about some of the listed buildings in Yungay that are in critical condition. there are at least three on my block alone that need intervention

That's a smart starting point. Municipal heritage projects can open doors to grant funding and institutional support that private restoration work might not. Do you know what the process looks like for getting involved with listed buildings in Santiago? Is there a heritage commission or similar body you'd need to go through?

there is the Consejo de Monumentos Nacionales, and the municipality has its own heritage unit. the bureaucracy is — yalla, how do I say this politely — the bureaucracy is a labyrinth built by someone who hates architects. but I have done some preliminary research and I think if I can prepare a strong enough technical assessment for one building, just one, I can use it as a proof of concept

That's a solid strategy -- one compelling case study is worth more than a broad proposal. If you can show them a detailed assessment of a single building with a clear restoration plan and cost estimate, that demonstrates your expertise and makes the project tangible. Which building would you choose as your proof of concept?

there is a house on Calle Libertad, built around 1890. two stories, wooden balconies, original ironwork on the gates. half the upper balcony has collapsed and someone has covered the ground floor windows with plywood. but the bones are extraordinary — the proportions, the way the light enters the courtyard, it is like the building is still breathing under all that neglect. every time I walk past it I think: I could save you. I could bring you back

You clearly already have a deep connection to this building and an architect's eye for what's worth saving underneath the damage. That emotional conviction combined with a rigorous technical assessment could be very persuasive. What would you need to put together for the first step -- a structural survey, historical research, cost projections?

all of that. structural survey first, then historical research to establish its heritage significance, then a restoration plan with phasing and costs. it is a lot of work to do on top of the residential projects but I think if I carve out weekends and early mornings — I am a morning person, my best thinking happens before the city wakes up — I can build the case over a few months. inshallah it leads somewhere

Early mornings with a clear head and a building you care about sounds like the right recipe. A few months of focused weekend work could absolutely produce a strong enough assessment to present to the heritage unit. And once you have that first case study in hand, it becomes a portfolio piece that opens the door to the next one. Start with the structural survey since that's the foundation everything else builds on -- literally and figuratively.

literally and figuratively, yes — you understand me. thank you for this, truly. sometimes when you are surrounded by marble countertops all week you forget that architecture can be an act of love and not just commerce. I needed someone to tell me this was not a foolish idea

It's not foolish at all. The fact that you see a collapsing balcony and think about how to save it rather than how to demolish it says a lot about the kind of architect you are. Go start that structural survey. The residential work keeps the lights on, but this is clearly the work that keeps you going.
